title: Taiwan computer chipmaker TSMC pledges another $100 billion to expand US chipmaking capacity
gist: Despite the headline referencing a TSMC $100B US pledge, the actual article text does not discuss that pledge at all; instead it explains the Section 48D Advanced Manufacturing Investment Credit under the CHIPS Act, which rose from 25% to 35% for property placed in service after December 31, 2025 but stops applying to construction starting after December 31, 2026, and has already helped catalyze over $540B in announced US semiconductor-supply-chain investment across 100+ projects in 28 states.

insights.claim: The article's title references a TSMC $100 billion US expansion pledge, but the actual body text contains no discussion of TSMC or that specific pledge — it is entirely about the Section 48D semiconductor manufacturing tax credit, indicating the posted text does not match its headline.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: Analysts citing this record for TSMC's $100B pledge should verify against the original source, since the collected text does not substantiate the headline claim.

insights.claim: The Section 48D Advanced Manufacturing Investment Credit rate increased from 25% to 35% for qualified semiconductor or semiconductor-equipment manufacturing property placed in service after December 31, 2025, but the enhanced rate does not apply to property whose construction begins after December 31, 2026.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: A hard, dated cliff (construction must start by Dec 31, 2026) to capture the 35% credit is a concrete near-term catalyst that should pull forward US semiconductor and semiconductor-equipment facility groundbreakings into 2026.
insights.quote: The credit rate increased from 25% to 35% for qualified property placed in service after December 31, 2025... Section 48D does not apply to property the construction of which begins after December 31, 2026.

insights.claim: The Section 48D credit has already helped catalyze more than $540 billion in announced US semiconductor-supply-chain investment across 100+ projects in 28 states, part of a broader roughly $1.76 trillion in announced US manufacturing investment tracked since 2025.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of US semiconductor reshoring investment already attributable to the tax credit ($540B across 100+ projects), a baseline figure for tracking incremental announcements as the 2026 construction-start deadline approaches.
insights.quote: The credit has already helped catalyze more than $540 billion in announced US semiconductor-supply-chain investment across 100+ projects in 28 states

insights.claim: The article argues that operational readiness, not just the tax credit, determines return on investment, since full staffing across announced US fab and supplier sites isn't expected until 2029 and beyond, and plants that instrument production from day one with real-time OEE and production data ramp faster and prove capacity sooner.
insights.speaker: user nl
insights.so_what: Highlights that the gap between a fab being 'built' and being 'productive' (staffing not complete until 2029+) is where investment returns are actually won or lost — a caution against assuming tax-credit-driven fab announcements translate quickly into output.
insights.quote: New US fabs and supplier facilities are coming online through 2026, yet full staffing across announced sites isn't expected until 2029 and beyond. That gap between built and productive is where returns are won or lost.
insights.locator: Why operational readiness decides the ROI
